item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really wanted to like this, and I did like some parts, but had to downvote it.

1. The "Distorted human mind trapped in things" is clichéd by this point.
2. There are some typos here and there that hurt the tone.
3. The skip is extremely long (without a real need to be).
4. You tell us EVERYTHING. I know some skips fail because they are way too "mysterious" and end up being incomprehensible but here I felt like you treated your readers like morons that wouldn't "get" anything unless they were explicitly told. This by itself would secure a downvote.
5. You didn't do anything original with the basic premise. The story you chose to tell has been done before with better results.

The basic idea is ok, but the skip needs some heavy rewriting and trimming to reach its full potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked the article, but as usual I've gotta nitpick a couple of the militaria things in it (not that I expect anyone will ever notice this).

  1. Pr. 941 Akula-class is technically the correct designation, but there's a bit of a mess surrounding that name so I'd recommend adding the NATO reporting name Typhoon-class. Summarized very briefly, in Russian the Pr. 941 is known as the Akula-class, but under the NATO naming system that refers to a completely different type of vessel, the Project 971 Shchuka-B attack submarine.
  2. Secondary to that, if put to sea in '87, it wouldn't be equipped with the RSM-56 Bulava missile, those are much newer and never used in service on a Typhoon. They carried the R-39 Rif/RSM-52 ballistic missile.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The file "gommunism.JPG" is not compliant with CC 3.0.

It has been taken down from its source material due to violating copyright laws, and has since been removed as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So good! It's a shame it's not as popular as it deserves to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really good but three things that kept bothering me was that at the beginning of the containment procedures, you said SCP-2956 was neutralized but later said it will be neutralized in around 56,000 years, I think "neutralized" should be capitalized, and in the note "unintelligible" should be changed to "illegible" (though that might just be my thoughts concerning using the two words). But I'll still give an upvote for SCP-2956-1 and SCP-2956-2 and for that note.

item.title: Nice
item.content:encoded: Nice work, dude! I knew you could do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This does a lot of things that I like. On the positive side, the interaction between the brothers was emotional and interesting to follow, the mix of biological-ish(shedding, hibernating, growing)/tehcnological/psychic elements of the submarine were fairly interesting, and I like a lot of the little procedural touches you used.

Unfortunately, there were still fairly frequent clinical tone problems and unrealistic dialogue, and, worst of all, you explain everything, in extensive detail, usually more than once. Not only that, these explanations are often serious violations of show-don't-tell. -2 just blurts out the entire motivation and broad method behind the creation of the SCP in a few paragraphs.1 You had someone in an interview log take the time to say, out loud, "Peter, you're sweating. You're hunched over and look like you're about to vomit." We get the implication that someone got turned into a car, so by god we have to have someone else confirm that.

There is just log after log of people just flat saying everything that's going on. It got to the point where I was so bored of information being presented that I just sort of skimmed the last two logs. Even the actual document between the logs was full of extra detail that didn't advance the story or add interest; much of the extra details in the containment procedures, for example.

I'm not saying everything has to run on mystery and redaction, but almost nothing is interesting enough to support this much documentation or stand up to this much scrutiny.

-1

(P.S. I didn't find this one being cliche as a problem, but towards that claim, we have a lot of GRU-P/Soviet contingency SCPs, including SCP-1984 and SCP-3034.)

Footnotes
1. The exact method may be lost, but that's nowhere near as important to us as it would be to the Foundation, and we even get tons of that in later notes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see what you mean by it being immersion breaking, but I guess I was just used to seeing quirky task force names regularly on the site. I've removed it anyway, as the edition of a new MTF felt quite unnecessary on another read seeing as there is already a perfectly usable MTF specialized in oceanic anomalies.

Is there anything else about it you didn't like, apart from the task force designation? Thanks for the critique anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure why one would go through so much trouble to mainstain tight and consistent tone through the procedures, clearly sticking to the tried-and-true SCP-writing method of keeping strict verisimilitude…and then give into including a goofy silly task force name.

Really takes one out of the work. Where before I was immersed enough to digest half a dozen paragraphs of procedures and protocol because the realism was on-point, I was immediately reminded in brutal fashion that this is just some random story cooked up by someone on the internet. I don't mind silly names and punny designations, but I think they have their place, and this isn't it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, my point regarding the transferal vs copying of consciousness is more a reflection of my personal take on the nature of consciousness and the continuity of mind rather than anything that the article explicitly does wrong.

with Ivan's brain literally placed into the submarine

So, if I understand correctly, this occurred after the action described by -2 in the first interview, namely "then they took his consciousness, removed it from his brain and placed it inside a computer". All I'm saying is that, in my eyes, the act of removing the consciousness from the brain and putting in a computer is itself an act which creates a new consciousness in the process, rather than preserving the old one. I see what you mean by the prolonged process of "moving" Ivan's consciousness around until it was eventually placed in a submarine, but it is the initial "removal" of consciousness from the brain that I consider an act of copying the mind, rather than transferring it, if you see what I mean.

I get the impression from the article that we differ in terms of our conceptions of consciousness, not that I'm a philosopher either. However, I feel that it is implied in the article that consciousness and body are ultimately separable objects, and although you do point out that Ivan's mind is shown to not be capable of living on a computer while remaining lucid, it still seems clear that my idea of consciousness contradicts the article's. Personally, consciousness is the emergent product of all the individual interactions of the brain's neurons, so that without them, the consciousness simply doesn't exist, or isn't the same thing anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't planning on going for a philosophical approach, but now I'm interested. Now, this is going to be quite ad hoc, so bare with me. I'm not sure where you get the idea of Ivan's mind being "copied", as the dialogue in the first interview states that they "took his consciousness" and placed it inside a computer. From a dualism point of view, the mind can exist without the body, but it's also implied in the dialogue that Ivan's mind wasn't able to exist inside a computer (without his body) so it's kind of "anti-dualism" I guess (correct me if my Philosophy is wrong). The guy in the recording also describes how parts of Ivan's body was added into the submarine to allow him to control it, with Ivan's brain literally placed into the submarine. This is also where his consciousness was centered, effectively lending the submarine almost complete autonomy if it weren't for Peter, and therefor making the submarine Ivan. I guess I didn't explain well enough how Ivan's mind was placed back into the submarine with the rest of his body to allow the submarine to operate.

Imagine it as a medical procedure, with Ivan's mind removed from his body to allow doctors to mix it with the submarine, with the mind being placed back once again and allowing it to operate with a brain (inside the submarine). Forgive me if it's long winded, I'll see if I can clean it up a bit.

Oh and btw, the mention of a "soul" in the final note is simply the man's religious side rearing its head in the note, and isn't meant to be taken as Ivan having a literal soul. And I completely agree about bits of the dialogue being too clunky, like the "etc" which really didn't work after a second reading.

Thanks for the critique anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm confused, does the submarine actually contain the brain of Ivan, or a digital copy of his consciousness? I understand that a central conceit of the article is that the submarine really is Ivan, based on the framing of the transformation of Ivan's mind and body. However, on a philosophical level, this skirts over questions regarding the nature of self and consciousness, which nagged at me while I was reading through. In my eyes, the submarine isn't Ivan, because based on what I read, his brain wasn't literally removed from his head, merely that it was copied, kind of like the brain scans from SOMA, so that -1 isn't Ivan, but merely a facsimile.

Besides this, the writing is very competent. However, I found the interviews, particularly the first, to be lacking. If the story must be presented through dialouge, then I assume a goal of this is to develop pathos. However, I don't have much sympathy for Peter, which is attributable to the blandness of his dialogue, which in turn makes him come off as a little nonchalant at times. For instance, when he says "he constantly tells me he's in pain, that it hurts, etc.", it's strange that he would literally say "etcetera" when describing the condition of his brother. One would assume that with such an intense relationship of sorts, characterised by a range of both positive and negative emotions, he would speak as if he were more invested that he seems to be. Ultimately, the interviews don't really help to convey the notion of a tragic connection between -1 and -2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unfortunately, my knowledge of Russianisms is limited to words like Comrade and Glasnost, which I've added in at a few points but very sparingly, as I feel it would be too forced and cliched. I've looked through some basic things on vocabulary in the Russian language, and I'm not sure how I can effectively inject it into the dialogue without making it even more clunky.

And about the dialogue: I wanted the character to have a specific arc of accepting his Brother in the end when he realized that the Foundation was planning on Killing him. I'm not too sure if I was able to convey that effective, but I can see what you mean about the dialogue not being completely consistent.

Perhaps also because it feels a little unsatisfying to have some undescribed, unforeshadowed walk-in deliver the epilogue.

I'm not too sure what you mean here. If you mean that it feels like he was delivering exposition, I can see what you mean, but I was trying to go for a kind of delusion-of-grandeur writing of someone who was planning their own suicide.

But I'm glad you liked some things anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

This one was a solid upvote from me. I enjoyed it quite a bit… it packed quite an emotional punch for me, almost everywhere. Plus, I have a thing for submarines.

Lengthy, but kept me enthralled, and worth the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I agree with most of these things. Although, I didn't feel that it was cliched, but that's probably because I haven't read that many 'soviets place someone's mind into a machine of war' skips/tales.

But I still upvoted because I found it really entertaining.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Promising: moments of flair and strong exploration of a relatively strong idea.

It is far too long for me, however. Much of what you have here feels like it's repeating the same ideas over and over. I didn't get much sense of progression from the interviews, for instance. You could easily have cut one of the three interview logs, and pared down the nightmare imagery to just the most evocative examples. The integration of submarine control with -1's proprioception was neat and plausibly described, but again, you could have boiled down all of that recording log to the most essential and effective elements.

For me, organisations, especially Soviets, putting consciousnesses inside military equipment is beginning to feel played out on here. (SCP-1984 is the most famous example.)

Dialogue varied in quality. I get the sense that you were very much writing from your own voice; I didn't hear a consistent, unique tone ringing out from -2, and amongst other things, his fluency seemed ill-fitting for a recent Soviet defector. You could have had fun sprinkling in a few Russianisms in vocabulary and grammar. Again, reducing the dialogue down to the best bits would have improved the varying quality. Some genuinely good bits here and there, though.

"Cyrillic written sentences" written on the walls by -1, and then very English language–specific typos – not sure how this works. Not written in Russian, right? Written in English but with Cyrillic letters? If so, clarify.

Last note didn't do much for me. I think it's partly because it was a little too long, so it didn't wasn't as punchy as it could have been. Perhaps also because it feels a little unsatisfying to have some undescribed, unforeshadowed walk-in deliver the epilogue.

There's plenty I did like: imagery (particularly the momentary appearance of that bust of Lenin), scientific realism, formatting, much of the core idea (biological submarine was fun to read about), scientific tone in the prose. Lots of promise, but would have really benefited from a lot of hard-nosed, aggressive editing.

Also made some SPaG edits. Feel free to overrule the full stops if you'd prefer it without.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As good now as when I critted, with the majority of issues I had with it addressed. Solid updoot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a riveting read from start to finish, with some solid weirdness and escalation.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aww, now I'm not the proud owner of the only page that mentions Žižek. Good scip though, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed. I'm glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice one! But just one thing: logo.png ain't working.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
I'm biting the bullet with this one and posting it. Many thanks to Hydrozen, Aurox and MaliceAforethought for looking over this in the forums.

Image credits:
All images are public domain, either because they were taken by the US navy or released under their creators.
